Hey, welcome to part three of Sales Mastery Unleashed, the No BS System for Closing Sales Like Crazy. In this next segment, you're going to learn precisely how to overcome objections and actually maybe even learn to love them. That's how powerful this next section is going to be. Now Nick, we're going to be using a lot from this, which is a bonus that comes with this system. But first, before we get to the actual scripting of objections, we're going to do a little role playing here. Dan, one of the things that you teach is that you should overcome objections in advance. Why? Most salespeople would say, well, I don't even want to bring up objections because I hope the prospect doesn't think of them. Well, see, the problem with that idea is that really stupid people generally don't have much money. So the person who is really so dumb that they are going to miss the flaws in the ointment probably can't afford to pay much for the ointment. So I'd rather sell to smart people any day of the week. So I think there's three things that are true. One is you want to have dealt with, at least not have, leave it to be a surprise, if not have fully erased as many objections as you possibly can, in advance of the actual sales event. So some are erased without people even realizing that's what they're doing. So somebody goes to a showroom to buy a Volvo. They're long past the objection that it's an ugly car with a horrible name. They're over that. Joan used to complain about it. So if a Jewish person goes to buy a Volkswagen, they are over the objection that it's a Nazi car. This has been taken care of sort of automatically. But often they're not automatic. They have to be thought through. So I will almost always prefer raising the curtain on the elephant lurking in the room on the theory that he can be smelled and better to paint him purple with yellow polka dots and trot him out and try and tell everybody why it's cool that you get the elephant as a bonus with the product. Or acknowledge it as damaging admission. But these 56 benefits far outweigh the fact that you have to wait eight weeks when you really want it tomorrow or whatever. Secondly, it's important to do it again in any kind of environment where the selling is not being entirely done by you face to face and you are highly skilled. So again, we can wait if we wish and can deal with every objection. If we employ 10 people, we're going to be in trouble. If we employ 100, it's going to be hopeless. If we sell at a distance, then we don't get human interaction. So we have to anticipate and answer. The last reason to do it is because it allows you to use your prime time, which is the face to face or on the phone closing of the sale, having to deal only with two or three instead of eight, 10, 12, 15 or 20. And answering objections turns into this ping pong game. And the prospect has a whole pocket full of ping pong balls. So he hits one over to you, you hit it off the board, he's got another one. And this goes on so long, nobody even remembers the sales presentation. They don't remember why they're there. Everybody's just exhausted. It's just not good. The narrower down to three or four things, you know we're going to be difficult. That you know you're going to have to deal with. But at least you've eliminated eight, 10, 12 other ones. That I think is beneficial. Obviously sales, there are going to be objections that come up that you may not have possibly, that you're not handling in advance. I think a lot of sales, in fact, I know a lot of salespeople, this is when they crumble, right? They get an objection, they either don't know how to answer it, they stumble, or they actually give up and say, oh, the person's not interested. Address that, Nick. It happens all the time. I've been on enough ride-alongs where you're with somebody and the whole way there, they're the best salesperson in the world. You sit down with the prospect, the prospect throws the first objection, they say, okay, thanks, and they get up, we're like, whoa, whoa, whoa, let's slow down a little bit. Because they think that the objection is really, I think it's more just part of the buying process. The person wants more information, or they may be schooled and selling and they want to test the salesperson. They want to see, well, they fold over. I do that. What's this person made of? They have moxie. I really think this is where salespeople that aren't schooled, that aren't trained, they really stumble because they think that it's a no when it's really just a request for more information or it's part of the romance. It's part of the sales process and we need to get to the end result. Or it's them telling you that you haven't sold yet. Yeah, I'm not sold yet. You haven't sold me. Again, the thing is, there doesn't have to be this problem because if you know, there's a set of objections, which we're going to go over, and there's a set of objections that almost everybody gets, but then there's specific objections to what you're doing, script them out. I mean, handle them, memorize them. That's what the pros do. So I'll give you two credit examples. So Ronald Reagan, when he ran against Mondale, most people won't remember Mondale. When Ronald Reagan ran against Mondale, there was the famous debate when Mondale rather foolishly brought up the age, you know, you're old and decrepit and dangerous, you know, sort of objection, more elegantly than I just did, but still. And Reagan said he was on the successive answer with us four or five times and I asked him about it. And he said, we really wish it had happened in the first debate because I didn't do very well in the first debate. And we were waiting for this question. He would die in for it. Just die in for it because we had a scripted, you know, zinger for it, you know, which he turned to Mondale and turned to the public and said, I'm not going to hold this man's youth and inexperience against him, right? And everybody laughed, including Mondale. And Mondale was scored. That was that point. It was like game over. So Carl and I are golden oaks, which is the big multi-million dollar home development that Disney is building. And we were there before there were even any finished models. So we're in a model that's like half done. And the back of it comes down in like four tiers of decking and foliage all the way down to the babbling brook, which is they put in, of course. But so, you know, there's a deck for your patio and then there's the spa and tub deck and then there's the barbecue. And finally, there's a seating looking at the beautiful babbling brook, right? Which Carl is kind of a water person. So she's excited. And I say, you don't spend that much time in Florida. I've spent a lot of time in Florida. You should know that four months out of the year where there's water, there's mosquitoes, there's a size of buicks. And if you are going to sit out here, you're going to sit wrapped in a big net and they're going to be bouncing off of it coming at you, right? And the real estate person says, oh, Mr. Kennedy, we don't have mosquitoes at Disney. This is Disney. You're screwed. At that point, Brian, just done. This conversation is over. Done. All she has to do is have anticipated, memorized it, and practiced delivering it with a straight face because it's hard to deliver that line with a straight face. And so many times it doesn't take much to make these things go away. People build up all sorts of negative anticipation and timidity and weakness about them. But really, it doesn't take that much to make them go away. Good. I think people assume when they come to an objection, there's a level of conflict. You were just making a statement. And she delivered it as best as it could be delivered. It was well done. She uses voice inflection, I'm sure. She touched you one of those fabulous. And we could all learn from that. And I think the salesperson will think that when a person just puts an objection out there, it is them saying no. It is not. It is just I need to keep on moving forward with the sales presentation. I realize this. I've heard it happen before. I've tested different objection re-bottles to that. And this is the one that works the best. And we're ready for the close after we achieve this objection. Yeah. And it's beautiful once you have it scripted because like you were saying, like Reagan, that's why I wasn't kidding. You can actually look forward to getting the objection because it helps you move the sale forward. It's exactly right. It advances the sale so far. So far when this one is raised and you deal with it expertly, that it's the best thing that could possibly happen. Right. And sometimes you actually script it in so you plant that up. I've done this. You plant the objection in the person's head so they actually do bring it up. So you can answer it. Again, this is not one-on-one selling. It's a little bit more advanced. Speaking of that, I'm going to ask you a question I didn't have written down. But I think it's an important one which could help everyone with not only handling objections and overcoming that, but with the whole sales process. How does psycho cybernetics fit into this whole thing? I mean, we can do a whole product on that, but I just want to touch upon it. Well, I mean, very simplistic way. Great salespeople have healthy self-images. So if you have a weak self-image, A, you are fearful of it being wounded because it's pretty weak to start with. And you are reluctant to be assertive because that invites possible confrontation. And you will be fearful of such things as objections because you will take it personally. So the weakest salespeople in the world take every sort of response other than, yes, has a personal affront and rejection. And those people generally can't prosper in a selling environment. They're putting things on to the prospect that really aren't even there. The prospect isn't thinking about them at all. He's not reacting to them at all. He's reacting to price, ability to afford price that he does not really want to disclose. He's reacting to factors, not to the person. So we did a Psycho-Sybernetics book. It's out of print, I think, but you'll probably find used copies of it on Amazon. Zero is the selling. Which is really all about the marriage of the inner game, not normal vits appeal, positive attitudes stuff. But I mean the inner game of how you see yourself and therefore how you see the selling environment and the prospect that you are interacting with. One of the simplest techniques from Psycho-Sybernetics and Dr. Malts is the theater of the mind. They should be practicing their presentation mentally as well as overcoming the objections. Can't do it without a script. Right. So you can't go into it. You can't go into the theater of your mind and play the movie of what's supposed to happen in the sales meeting we're about to have if I haven't scripted the sales meeting in the first place. Right, I haven't scripted the answers to the objections and all of that. But if you do that, man, that is so powerful. I mean I do that before I speak. I go through, this is how it's going to go, this is how it's going to happen. So you're addressing a bigger issue that is important to this whole program. And for anybody who doesn't yet own it thinking about buying. See most people get very poor results with selling and actually in their lives because they are very casual about all that they do. So the person who for example takes an unscheduled incoming call from a prospect. He's not prepared in general to handle such a call at that moment in time. He's definitely not prepared for the specific prospect to whom he is going to speak. And so he's going to get not very good results. Prepare for, prepared for, planned and timed calls during a day than 40 unprepared for. So this really is about taking a prepared approach, a non-casual, serious approach to every aspect of selling including your own inner game that you apply to. Absolutely. And we're going to be talking about that on the bonus call that we do which is coming up. We're going to be going into the mental game of this as well. So do you have something else to say? Yeah, you know we were talking about that and we were talking a little bit about it at dinner. You said when I first started and I had a right copy for my own business, I had to become the best copywriter possible. And I said when I first started selling for one of my own businesses, I had to be the best salesperson out there because Chase didn't care how many visits I had or how many meetings I had. They wanted dollars in the bank to pay the mortgage. And so it really comes to the approach of the only objections are in your mind. And the timidness of a salesperson. Zigg always said timid salespeople have skinny kids. And so you really have to become professional. You really have to become a pro. You run a forward focus on scripts and think through all the objections and move through the sales process and become the best you can. And in the theater of mind, I always replayed on the way there a positive sales meeting. And on the way back, if it didn't go that well, because they all don't go that well, I don't have Dan's skill. But on the way back, I would play what happened, what went wrong and it tests different things. So you say I said this, I noticed their reaction, how do I adjust from those reactions and re-script myself. So I would go over and over and over and over them because the penalty of failing was so high. I just couldn't afford to. I had burnt all the ships. And I really, really, it had to be successful. And that's how I approach their sales position after that. It has to be successful. And that's what you look for in your salespeople. And I think pros don't win it. They don't leave it to chance. They don't let the little things get in their way. They really step forward. And that could come in your interview process for the salesperson. But sometimes when you're scaling, you can't catch everything. And when you get to 100, you're going to get some people. So you really want to take everything along the way and have a systematic process and a script that helps drive for success. Yeah. And just so if you're wondering, we keep talking about this bonus training that Nick and I are going to do. Since we're recording this live, obviously if you've bought this already and you're watching this, well, you've got the bonus CDs right in your package. So again, if you've got the package in your hand and you're watching this, just go to your package and that's already in there. Okay. So there are a handful of objections that almost everybody gets. And I do want to go, we're going to do a little role playing here with one of the bonuses that comes with this package. But let's talk about the core ones that almost everybody has and how we address them. Now, we're not going to do an actual script here because the actual scripting is in this bonus report. But let's talk about price first. It costs too much. Well, so here's the most interesting thing about that is if you've done a competent job of value to price and specific relevance and so forth, then that objection is almost always a why. It is really a mask over something that is either uncomfortable for them to express or that they really don't even know how to enunciate. So it is often a mask over, I can't afford it, right? Not that the price is too high. So hardly anybody, for example, would argue that the price for a Bentley, certainly after they've driven it, is too high. But there's a whole lot of people who cannot afford a $400,000 automobile. And there's a whole lot of people who would just feel really bad about buying a $400,000 automobile for themselves. So there's I can't afford it. I would feel horrible about spending that much money on an indulgence when there are starving people in Botswana, when there are stray cats with no homes, whatever. So this is often a way of them kind of fumbling around, right? Fumbling for help was something that they cannot enunciate or are not going to enunciate. And people often read these things wrong in face-to-face selling, often in his case because they're in too much of a damn hurry. And because they're not scripted. So they are having to think about what they're going to say. So they can't even really stop and think about, well, what does this guy really mean? Right? It is very memorable. And it was representative of a mistake that happens all the time when I was doing a lot of work with this hearing aid company. One of the sales interactions that we had taped and transcribed is at the point of the guy said, so this is like a $4,000 to $8,000 sale. He said, gee, I certainly wasn't planning on spending that much money on myself. Now he actually enunciated. The real reason. The real reason. The salesman went to price value. And he went back. Did he handle a real objection? He went back into, it's made from titanium and it costs a fortune for the molds and the NASA uses the same. So he went back into all that stuff. The guy who was in his 80s, of course, is really saying, I got grandkids. I feel morally obligated. They get at that age, if you give them a sweater for Christmas, they take it down to get a refund. They put the money back in the box for the grandkids for when they die. I mean, that's just right. So often a price objection, even when it's said as a price objection, it's not really an objection to price. It has a different meaning. And conversely, often they'll actually state it right and a poorly skilled, non-listening salesperson will turn it into a price objection. Yeah, absolutely. And the other thing is something that you do very well is pre-framing before the prospect even gets in front of you, they know it's not going to be cheap. Yeah, they don't have to know the exact price necessarily. They know that. There is a way to sell where they know the exact price. But they at least know by who they are, where they were sourced from, what they have seen in advance. And in some cases, it's stated without a number. They know that this is not going to be, it's called price desensitization, and it happens all the way along a process. You mentioned Charlie, when you walk in the dental office, you know this ain't asperent. And really what you say to yourself is, oh, this is going to cost a little more than I thought it was. So now when the price is a little more than you had prepared yourself for, up until then, you say, oh yeah, I told myself that 22 minutes ago, you're sort of prepared for it. And then the other thing is, if you're getting that a lot and it really is price, well then you're bringing in the wrong prospects. And your marketing and your prospecting is not bringing in the right folks. Market to message match. You're just bringing in the wrong folks. That's why I really don't personally, I don't like the standard canned answers to the price objection. Because like you said, there's typically something more to it than it's actually too much money. Nick, let's go to the next one. And again, this kind of goes to the same answer to the first one. Now by the way, in a typical sales trading course, they would give you the, you know, here are the five things to say, but this is going a little bit deeper than that. No need. No need. You know, I think of this one you're selling against status quo. So some of it could be the objections, you haven't covered the objection or your presentation didn't build enough need or want in the person. We talked in the first or second segment about pain and pleasure. So you didn't move them to something or you didn't move them away from a level of pain. And so they haven't had the disconnect of not having the product. And so I really think it's really built into the presentation themselves. And I like to use pain and I think you're a big fan of it also, you know, whether it's agony or anything of that nature of moving them away from... You mean like pain and selling? Pain and selling. Okay, I just want to clarify that. Thank you. Thank you. But moving them... He's like very serious. He's like... I have to be. I have a friends' cup all afternoon and it's filled with bourbon so you know I'm trying to work it through. But you know moving them either away from pain or towards pleasure. And I think at the end of the day, that is where the no need comes from. What happened to be in a... Our poor people live like rich people and the rest of them are... Right. I mean there's an enormous number of people you are going to sell to who really don't need anything. They could go a long time, right? Our closets are all full. We don't really need to buy anything. Most of us are probably going to buy some clothes this year, but the truth of the matter is to make room for them. We've got to take something and give it to the goodwill just to be able to put it in the closet. But even so, the higher up the affluence level you go in selling, the less anybody needs anything. So you have to not have this in play at all, right? It has to be about want. It has to be about, even though I've done some, I've tried to solve this problem 18 times before and I have 18 things that I own that have failed to solve it, this finally is the thing that will make that solution go away. But even sort of at the bottom of the economic level. So Walmart's whole business, particularly their superstores that have food, is about people buying things they don't need on impulse to feel a little better about their circumstances when they come to buy the basics. That's their whole business. The only profit is in the DVD and the toy and the extra kitchen organizer and that that people buy when they come to get toilet paper and ground meat and four cans of soup and they spend an extra half an hour in the store because that's free entertainment and they buy four or five things and they feel better about them. They don't need any of them. Most people, even them who buy a DVD, have DVDs that aren't there and watched yet. Still a rapper. But it's still a rapper. They don't need it. They got a television and they got Netflix for crisis and they're getting a DVD out of Red Box on the way out to talk. So the no need issue is really a failure of salesmanship. I mean that's really what it is because most people buy enormous quantities and diversity of things that they do not actually need. The truth is people buy what they want, not necessarily what they need. So I mean talking about, I keep buying mentalism books. Well, that's collecting, which is a whole category. Yeah, but I want it though. I don't need it. So I just bought a whole bunch of new DVDs. I'm like, I don't do one damn trick from these 50 other DVDs I bought. I don't need it, but I want it because they make me want it. And so again, really important. Okay, here's a good one. All right, I've got to talk to my butcher baker, candlestick maker, my wife, my mistress, whoever it is. How do we handle that? Part of that goes back to bad salesmanship that Dan and I have all talked about. And maybe we didn't set it up properly from the beginning. And I think that happens. That's lazy selling or that's the salesperson rushing to a prospect that doesn't have the ability to buy. And if you have to have all the people in the room, then you need to get all the people in the room. I mean, it's as simple as that. Right, why are you doing the presentations? Why are you exactly right? So we talked chiropractors for years. In prepay, it's one thing if it's pay as you go. But if it's prepay and the end of a case presentation is you're going to commit to $14,000 worth of care. In most families, spouse, that's going to have to be discussed. So the only way to handle this objection in that environment is the spouse has to be there. They have to be. So it has to be a mandatory part of the report of findings presentation. And if they object to that, you can't have the presentation. There's no need to go forward. Let me give you the name of four or five other doctors. But not here. This is how we do it. And that gets to having enough lead flow. To all sorts of things. But he's a thousand percent right. If there is a person who is going to muck around and they are going to, I just had this happen with me and it's people who should know better. Selling me, attempting to sell me a very sophisticated and exotic financial instrument. And so they went through all the gyrations of getting this appointment, which is not easy. Then they want to do the call with us looking at go to meetings, which I'm not going to do. So they've got to send me what I would see on the screen or on paper before we get on the phone and all this. This is a very complicated purchase. It's an expensive purchase. You have to have a significant net worth to even be allowed to make the purchase in the first place. Now if you can't anticipate that the stall slash objection to this purchase is going to be, I have to speak to my CPA and my tax attorney and my financial advisor before I do this, you must be making the presentation for the first time. So why would you let that happen? Because all attempts to overcome it are going to be horribly pushy. There are no scripted attempts there. There are, but you try and turn it into a conditional purchase. You try and you know. But really, it should have been handled before that. It should have been a conference call. Everybody should have been on the call. And all the CPA can't get, well, I'm not going to finish the call. We'll rebook it for two weeks from now. Mr. Prospect, don't screw it up again or I'm not even going to let you rebook it at all. I mean you just have to have this handled properly. Yeah, absolutely. And I think Dan hit it on a couple of quick points that we're not covering in this, but really it's lead flow, right? So it's the last five times perfectly with magnetic marketing and ultimately generation machine. Yeah, because the lazy salesperson or the desperate salesperson thinks, just get me in front of somebody and I'm going to get them closed. Well, and I have to. Right. And they're, and it's just not going to work. And it's going to be, it's going to be unfortunate for everyone to say the least. And it's going to be bumpy and ugly. And it's just not. So you have to be in a position where you know, not hope, not by positive attitude, have faith in, you have to know there's another bus coming. Right. So for some reason, this bus and I are not, get away from the bus stop because there's another bus coming. And if you don't have yourself in that position as a salesperson, then you are always going to surrender effective selling in favor of desperation. Next objection is, and we've actually covered this one right here, this won't work for me. This won't work in my business. That's one that we hear all the time at GKIC again. Well, the consumer versions are, this won't work for me because we're Catholic, we're Mormon, we're fat, we're old, we're young. We live in the South, we live in the Midwest. Yeah. So again, this is something that in my mind should be handled way in advance. I mean, if you're getting this objection during a presentation, I mean, shouldn't you be handling this in advance? I think it's best to handle the testimonials. So if you know who they are, you would say, these are doctors that use this. If the person says, it's not going to work for my medical practice. Or that's really where it's proof and abundance with your testimonials is the best way to do that. It goes back to really taking the time and the thought to work your way through the script. So there's a marketing before selling opportunity to match people with social proof, corporate proof, whatever. So in selling Oklahoma, one of the kinds of people who would not normally want to locate in Oklahoma. Excellent from the big city, like New York City or like Tech Guys. However, Google has a big plant there. So to them, you should be telling the Google story. To retailers, Macy's has a big distribution center there. And Amazon has a big distribution center. So the sloppy thing is to show everything to everybody, which is woefully ineffective. Because I'm most influenced by somebody who looks like me, talks like me, walks like me, etc. So there's a way to start to deal with this long before it happens in selling. In the sales place, you better be ready. You better be able to produce that social proof as it is needed to match to that person. What about hidden objections? Let's talk a little bit about one of them, which is I believe these testimonials. I believe that you're the real deal, but I don't believe in myself. I don't trust in myself. I think very few people are going to come out and say, hey, I don't think I can do it. How do we handle that as a hidden objection? I think that's valid. I mean, a lot of times people will not trust themselves to implement. If it's a marketing campaign, they may not think that they could implement all 52 steps. Is it a diet? Is it diet? They think they won't stick to it. You have to think about it. They are plagued with their history of positive and negative, but mostly they're thinking about their failures. The most orienteering. Right. And it's the diets that didn't work. The ghosts of disappointments passed. The ads that didn't pull. Always come running out of the closet. And I think that's valid. And I think the best way to do it is take them off the hook for that. And you can say, I realize in the past maybe so-and-so didn't go the way we wanted to, but these are the reasons why we've created this full proof mechanism. And I think you have to take them off the hook. You have to tell them it's not their fault about the past in a way that it's appropriate. And then you have to just move forward. But it is, I think of all the valid objections, it's probably one of the most valid. So I'm writing a copy right now for Roxanne Emmerich. Who are you? I'm not sure. Titanium member. Titanium member. Minnesota. And deals with bankers. Bank presidents, bank CEOs. With complete culture transformation. Really every aspect of the thing. And I'm anticipating objections because I'm selling at a distance. And one of them is this. We got a closet full of training programs. We've run consultants through here. We've done all of that. And we know, truth is, we won't implement. It's actually easier to raise it at a distance than it is one to one. This happens to be one that if you put it in the 12 reasons not to buy, the individual never really has to enunciate it to spot it. And well, I'm home alone. Nobody's going to know. I'll read the answer to this. So you can be pretty blunt and frank and candid about this because you're not confronting face to face. But I think it's not there. Of course it's there. They know. I was doing a consultation years ago. I was at Amway Corporation in Grand Rapids in a board room with 30, 35 executives. And the meeting was all about them doing infomercial style DVDs for the more expensive stuff they sell to leave. So Joe Distributor who can't sell won't sell, etc. Just leaves the DVD with you. It sells the fire alarm. It sells the cookware. And one of the executives said an extremely perceptive thing. So one spoke up and said, well, we really only need the strategic help with this because we have a video studio that rivals Hollywood. We produce video all the time. And the other executive spoke up and said, if we do it in a house, we'll Amwayize it and we'll wind up with exactly the same thing we have now. We will fuck it up. We need to get it out of here in the hands of entirely different people. And so he was willing to admit we are going to implement this. And so certainly with consumers with weight loss, with health, with fitness, there's a lot of that. And you're exactly right. They have to be let off the hook. The blame has to go on the previous stuff, not on them. And there has to be an answer to why this is different. Absolutely. Well, the last one in this section, boy, we're running up against the clock on this one. But here's a classic. You know what it is. You know what we love it. I have to think about it. I've got to think about it. Rick? Sure. What do you need to think about? It is. The smart aleck in me. But I would say, you know, it goes back to the sales presentation. So there isn't an impetus to buy now. And maybe it's the bonuses that go away from the people or premiums that go away to make them act now. You want to move them towards really what their objective is, is the benefit that they're moving forward or they're moving away from. And you know, some people need to think. They need to ponder. They just need to come up with it on their own mind. I don't want to leave it to those people. I want to make sure that we do a conditional sale. Let's move forward with this. And then if you think about it, just at the end of the day, isn't for you. We're going to move away from it. But it goes back to they don't trust themselves. They're replaying past movies in their mind that weren't successful and you need to move yourself through that. There's a town, think it over Iowa. It has only a yellow traffic light. It's where all these people go right after they've told you to think it over as they go back home to think it over Iowa. So look, there are some purchases matched with some people that this is a legitimate thing. If you sell in rural Midwest and rural South, you will encounter evangelical Christians who have an oath that they do not make any sort of decision without going home and praying over it. They're telling the truth and acceptable response is usually not let's all get down on our knees and play together. There are people who pull that off, but not many. You wouldn't be one to pull that off. No. There are people who have an oath amongst themselves to sleep. So this is real. And you're not going to dissuade them. There are complicated purchases. More often than not, though, you've either bungled the lead up to it. So if someone walks into your high-end furniture store off the street and they have gone out shopping on the weekend on their own, they may not have measured their spaces with a tape measure and brought their information because they said to themselves, we're just looking this weekend. So unless you exercise preparatory control and they were leads and they responded to lead generation and they booked an appointment and one of the things that they were told to bring with them and you sent them a tape measure to do it with. So you can't preempt it with everybody, but you could preempt it with some of it. So a lot of it is you sort of screwed up the lead in to the process one way or the other. Then sort of tactically to where he went, you need to have a really good urgency case by reason and by bribe, by incentive for moving quicker than you are than the prospect perhaps was initially prepared to do. And it's best if you have the ability to drop down to a truly conditional sale that, so I had a client for years in selling vacation homes and sites on which you would build. And so the drop down to pay it for a site right now was you really like this site because of the view of the lake and whatever, you can make a deposit for first right or refusal. And now for 90 days, if somebody else wants it, we're delaying them by a day, we're calling you and you will have first opportunity to try. So you can start to do these kind of conditional things. And the other thing is, which really takes it out of this program, but if you have much of this, you should have a really, really good follow up system for the non-buyer. Because there is gold there, they will come back, they can be brought back. And in many cases, it'll be over a period of time that will surprise you. Gotcha. You know, another tactical strategy is to find out, because you could often find out in a more elegant way than what you have to think about, is to use an NLP, what we call the agreement frame. So you can say, you know, I understand that you need to think about it. But it's a clarifying my thinking, what specifically is it that you're going to think about? Because oftentimes they'll say, well, it's this and then that's the real objection. So you can actually get to the real objection as well. Let's see. Oh man. Okay. I mean, Jim Rohn tells a, I mean, I'm not trying to be a wise guy, but Jim Rohn tells a great story about how he went out and he did great presentations. And people would say, you're the best salesman I've ever seen. That was the best presentation. And he'd come back to the office and you'd say, I'm the greatest sales person. But wait a minute. How come I'm not making any sales? Right? So, I mean, it really comes to the fact of you've got to get to the decision maker. This is what we talked about earlier. You're selling to the wrong people. You're not selling. You want yourself be put in a position where the decision maker is not present. You're not selling. You're a professional visitor making friends. Right. Right? You're passing out candy or cookies. I mean, so you went and you spent four hours in a boardroom with six people. But the guy who asked to make the decision wasn't there, you had a nice social activity and they like you. Relying on them to go make a sale for you is hopeless. And so, it's very hard to get people to do this because they confuse activity with accomplishment.
